step_by_step_reasoning: The architecture is split into deployment and invocation phases. In the deployment phase, developers use a CLI (UserCompanyDeveloper) to push code to a scalable deployment service running on EC2. This service records deployment states in DynamoDB and pushes jobs into SQS to manage rate limits. A backend EC2 worker polls SQS to safely deploy the code as Lambda functions in a secure, isolated AWS account. In the invocation phase, end-users using browsers (UserConsumerWeb) access an invocation service on EC2 which performs auth checks and executes the targeted customer Lambda using secure V8 isolates.

graph.name: Atlassian Forge Extensibility Platform
graph.notes: Atlassian Forge uses a multi-tenant Lambda execution model with V8 isolates inside a dedicated AWS account to secure tenant code execution.

nodes.name: Developer CLI
nodes.notes: Developers write and deploy custom application code using the CLI.

nodes.name: Deployment Web Service
nodes.notes: Receives and scales deployment requests from the developer CLI.

nodes.name: State Database
nodes.notes: Stores and tracks the progress and state of active deployments.

nodes.name: Deployment Queue
nodes.notes: Buffers deployment requests to prevent rate limiting of the Lambda control plane.

nodes.name: Deployment Worker
nodes.notes: Consumes deployment jobs from SQS and creates customer Lambdas.

nodes.name: Customer Lambdas
nodes.notes: Runs tenant code in dedicated AWS account using secure V8 isolates.

nodes.name: End User Browser
nodes.notes: Interacts with Forge applications and triggers function execution.

nodes.name: Invocation Service
nodes.notes: Handles authentication, permission checks, and triggers Lambda execution.

edges.notes: Deploys custom code as Lambda function.
